title: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR REFRIGERATED PACKAGED FOODS WITH EXTENDED SHELF LIFE

sections.section: CODE OF HYGIENIC PRACTICE FOR REFRIGERATED PACKAGED FOODS WITH EXTENDED SHELF LIFE
sections.summary: Document title only: 'Code of Hygienic Practice for Refrigerated Packaged Foods with Extended Shelf Life' (non-substantive heading).
Document reference code only: CXC 46-(1999) (non-substantive identifier).

Table of contents only, listing the code's sections (introduction, objectives, scope, definitions, production, establishment design, control of operation, maintenance, personal hygiene, transport, labelling, training, appendix).

Sets the code's purpose to provide HACCP-based recommendations for processing, packaging, storage and distribution of refrigerated packaged foods with extended shelf-life to prevent outgrowth of pathogens; notes refrigeration (e.g., +4°C) as an important but guideline-level hurdle, advises use of additional hurdles, predictive models and, where needed, challenge studies under worst-case storage/distribution to confirm effectiveness and determine appropriate shelf life.

Sets out the Code’s scope, use and definitions for heat‑treated low‑acid (pH>4.6, aw>0.92) refrigerated packaged foods with extended shelf life (>5 days), lists product characteristics, examples and exclusions, requires use with the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene, and provides key definitions.

Specifies the Code applies to heat‑treated, low‑acid (pH>4.6, aw>0.92) packaged foods intended to be refrigerated with extended shelf life (>5 days), possibly using additional hurdles, and which may or may not require reheating before consumption.

States this Code follows and must be used together with the Codex International Code of Practice – General Principles of Food Hygiene and provides recommendations specific to safety of refrigerated packaged foods with extended shelf life.

Provides definitions and cross‑references for terms used in the Code (e.g., container, cooling equipment, filling and sealing, hermetically sealed container, High Risk area, hurdle/hurdle technology, modified atmosphere, pasteurization value and D/z values, rapid cooling, refrigerated food/facility, shelf life, and use‑by date) and refers to the General Principles for additional definitions.
Non‑substantive cross‑reference heading that refers readers to the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene for primary production guidance.

Covers design and facilities for areas where refrigerated packaged foods are prepared, cooked, chilled and stored, requiring segregation of High Risk areas, hygienic layout and fittings, dedicated equipment, temperature monitoring and alarms, provision for rapid cooling and sufficient refrigeration capacity, filtered positive‑pressure air in HR areas, appropriate drainage and sanitation measures to prevent contamination, and references the General Principles where applicable.
Cross‑reference only — directs readers to the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene for location requirements.

Requires premises and room design, especially in High Risk areas, to minimise contamination and ease cleaning (including one‑way flow and FIFO storage), and specifies internal fittings and controls (e.g., non‑opening windows/close‑fitting doors, changing/hand‑washing facilities, temperature monitoring, filtered positive‑pressure air and humidity/condensation control).

Requires equipment in HR areas to be dedicated and cleaned/disinfected before entry, segregated for handling heat‑treated products, hygienically designed and located to prevent cross‑contamination, with monitoring/calibration of control instruments and reference to appropriate waste containers.

Addresses facilities (water, drainage, cleaning, personnel hygiene, temperature control, refrigeration, cooling, air quality and storage) with requirements such as cloakrooms/toilets not opening directly into food areas, preference for hands‑free taps, monitored/recorded refrigeration with alarms, rapid‑cooling capability sized to production, treated filtered air under positive pressure in HR areas, and properly routed drainage and waste piping to minimise contamination.

Requires establishment‑specific control procedures developed using HACCP to ensure safety of refrigerated packaged foods across varied raw materials and processes, assigns overall responsibility to qualified personnel, and requires shelf life be determined from scientific data.

Mandates application of Codex HACCP principles to identify hazards, set critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions and recordkeeping; and requires scientific establishment and validation by qualified personnel of product shelf life, scheduled heat or other treatments (calculated for the coldest point and worst‑case), cooling methods and use of additional hurdles.

sections.section: 5.2 Key aspects of hygiene control systems
sections.summary: Specifies time‑temperature hygiene controls—avoid or rapidly pass through 60→10°C (critical growth range), define and control thawing, quantify and monitor lethality of heat/other treatments with calibrated recording equipment and indicators, monitor cooling, maintain and validate the cold chain and storage temperatures (including meeting regulatory limits), and perform stock rotation and temperature monitoring throughout storage and transport.
Non‑substantive pointer indicating recommendations for incoming materials are provided in Section 5.3.

Defines refrigerated packaged foods with extended shelf life (>5 days) as heat- or otherwise-treated products that are not commercially sterile, states the manufacturer is responsible for ensuring safety throughout shelf life accounting for temperature abuse and psychrotrophic pathogens (e.g., Listeria, some C. botulinum strains), and notes that additional hurdles (e.g., reduced pH or aw, preservatives) may be needed; also warns that MAP can suppress spoilage indicators and permit pathogenic growth if not properly controlled.

States the Code's objective to provide recommendations for processing, packaging, storage and distribution of refrigerated packaged foods with extended shelf life to prevent pathogen outgrowth using HACCP principles, notes refrigeration is an important but guideline-level hurdle (e.g., ~+4°C), and requires use of predictive models and, where evidence is lacking, worst-case challenge studies to confirm chosen hurdles and determine safe shelf life.

sections.section: 2 SCOPE AND USE OF THE DOCUMENT
sections.summary: Combined scope and use: the Code addresses low-acid, heat-treated refrigerated foods susceptible to pathogen outgrowth during extended shelf life (products intended to be refrigerated, >5 days, pH>4.6, aw>0.92, packaged and possibly requiring additional hurdles), lists examples and exclusions, and directs that the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene must be used together with this Code for specific recommendations.

Specifies the Code covers low-acid (pH>4.6), high water activity (aw>0.92) refrigerated foods that are heat treated or otherwise reduced in microbial load, intended to be refrigerated with shelf life >5 days, may use additional hurdles, are packaged (not necessarily hermetically), and may or may not require reheating before consumption.

Use note: this Code follows the structure of the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene and must be applied together with that Code, providing recommendations specific to safety of refrigerated packaged foods with extended shelf life.

Definitions and references: refers to the Codex General Principles and defines terms relevant to refrigerated packaged foods and control measures, including container, cooling equipment, filling and sealing, hermetically sealed container, High Risk (HR) Area, hurdle and hurdle technology, modified atmosphere, packaging materials, pasteurization value and D/z values, rapid cooling (passing 60°C–10°C quickly), refrigerated food/facility, shelf life and use-by-date.
Cross-reference: primary production recommendations are covered by the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (no additional content here).

sections.section: Refer to the International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene.
sections.summary: Cross-reference entry indicating that primary production and establishment design/facility requirements rely on the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene and that recommendations on incoming materials are given in Section 5.3.

sections.section: For recommendations relative to incoming materials see Section 5.3.
sections.summary: Pointer only: recommendations concerning incoming materials are provided in Section 5.3.

Establishment design and facilities: refers to the General Principles and mandates measures to prevent contamination including segregation of High Risk (HR) areas, one-way flow and FIFO storage, dedicated and cleaned equipment for HR areas and heat‑treated products, appropriate drainage and waste controls, hygiene facilities (cloakrooms, hand‑washing/disinfection), and controls for temperature—with monitoring/alarms—rapid cooling capacity, air filtration/positive pressure, calibrated equipment and effective steam/humidity control to minimize cross-contamination.
Cross-reference: location requirements are addressed in the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (no additional details provided here).

Premises and rooms: refers to the General Principles and states that HR areas should be designed to minimize contamination, facilitate cleaning/disinfection, follow one-way flow and first‑in‑first‑out principles, and maintain appropriate temperature, humidity and ventilation.
Cross-reference: design and layout recommendations are contained in the Codex General Principles of Food Hygiene (no substantive text here).

Internal structures and fittings for HR areas: require entrance cleaning/changing facilities for shoes and protective clothing, hand‑washing and sanitizing stations, non‑opening windows and close‑fitting doors, temperature monitoring/recording with alarms, filtered air under positive pressure, and effective steam removal and humidity control to minimize condensation and cross‑contamination.

Internal structures and fittings (duplicate): reiterates HR area requirements—cleaning/changing facilities, hand‑washing/sanitizing stations, sealed windows and close‑fitting doors, temperature monitoring with alarms, filtered positive‑pressure air, and effective steam/humidity control to reduce contamination and condensation.

Specifies internal structure and fitting requirements for High Risk (HR) areas: provide entrances with shoe/changing facilities and handwashing/sanitizing stations; windows fixed and doors close‑fitting; where appropriate install temperature monitoring/recording with alarms; maintain filtered, positively pressured air; and ensure hygienic, well‑maintained steam removal and humidity control systems to minimize condensation and cross‑contamination.

Requires in HR areas that all food‑control apparatus be regularly checked and calibrated to an established procedure, processing equipment be located to prevent cross‑contamination between raw and processed products, and processing apparatus be hygienically designed and fitted with suitable instrumentation.

sections.section: 4.3.3 Containers for waste and inedible substances
sections.summary: Section is a cross‑reference to the International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene and contains no substantive requirements on waste or inedible‑substance containers itself.

Facility requirements reference the General Principles of Food Hygiene and set specific obligations: HR‑area drainage must flow via traps to a main drain and wastewater from refrigeration/handwashing/machinery be piped to avoid product contamination; cloakrooms/toilets must not open into food areas and hand‑disinfection taps should preferably be non‑hand‑operated; plants must be designed to control interior temperatures to limit microbial growth and refrigerated rooms require visible temperature monitoring/alarms and adequate cooling capacity for rapid chilling and storage equal to at least daily production; ventilation/air treatment must prevent dust and condensation and HR areas require filtered, positively pressured air, while lighting and storage follow the general code.
This section is a reference to the International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene and contains no additional substantive text on water supply.

In HR areas drainage must flow directly to a main drain via suitable traps (other area drains must not flow through HR drains) and wastewater from refrigeration, handwashing and machinery should be piped to the drainage system to minimize product contamination, with attention to splashing/aerosol.
This section is a cross‑reference to the International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene and contains no substantive cleaning requirements itself.

Refers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and specifies that cloakrooms/toilets should not open directly into food handling areas and that hand‑disinfection stations in processing areas preferably use taps that do not require hand operation.
States the obligation that cloakrooms and toilets must not open directly into any food handling areas.
Recommends that hand‑disinfection stations in processing areas use taps that do not require hand operation to reduce contamination risk.

Requires plant design to maintain interior temperatures that control microbial proliferation regardless of outside temperature, mandates refrigerated rooms have temperature monitoring and alarms, and requires establishments to provide rapid cooling capability and refrigerated storage sized for at least the maximum daily production with cooling equipment selected to allow refrigeration without delay after heat treatment (noting the 60°C internal temperature reference) and even temperature distribution; rapid chilling of cooked (unfilled/unsealed) products must be in equipment/rooms designed to prevent contamination.

All refrigerated rooms must be fitted with temperature monitoring and recording devices and a reliable audible or visual alarm system, with devices clearly visible and positioned to accurately record the maximum temperature in the area.

Establishments must provide facilities that permit rapid cooling and refrigerated storage at least equal to maximum daily production, and select cooling equipment based on product characteristics and production quantities to enable immediate refrigeration after heat treatment (noting the internal 60°C reference) and even temperature distribution during cooling.

High Risk areas must be designed to minimize contamination accumulation and maximize ease of cleaning, and storage/processing facilities should follow one‑way flow and first‑in‑first‑out principles while being equipped to maintain appropriate temperature, humidity and ventilation.

Referring to the General Principles of Food Hygiene, it requires that in HR areas the incoming air be treated to remove dust, ventilation be designed to prevent condensation and dust circulation, and air be filtered and maintained under positive pressure.

States that rapid chilling of cooked (not filled/sealed) products must occur in rooms/equipment designed and operated to prevent contamination, and reiterates HR‑area air requirements: treated to remove dust, ventilation preventing condensation/dust circulation, and filtered, positively pressured air.
This section is a reference to the International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene and contains no additional substantive lighting requirements itself.
This section is a reference to the International Code of Practice - General Principles of Food Hygiene and contains no additional substantive storage requirements itself.

Covers overall operational control for refrigerated packaged foods: hazards vary by product so each establishment must define product‑specific procedures to ensure safety, assign overall responsibility to qualified personnel and apply HACCP principles to determine controls and shelf life based on scientific data, including validation of heat/cooling/preservation steps and record‑keeping; manufacturers must develop and document procedures accounting for plant‑specific conditions, processes and product characteristics.

Mandates application of Codex HACCP principles to identify hazards, establish control measures, critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions, verification and record keeping; requires qualified personnel to set product shelf life and design elements (heat/other treatments, cooling methods and additional hurdles) using scientific/technological methods and validation (including calculating required log reductions for the coldest point, considering worst‑case scenarios, and recommended cooling to under 10°C at product center within two hours where feasible).

Requires processors to apply Codex HACCP principles to all existing and new products, identify hazards and controls, establish operational controls, critical limits, monitoring, corrective actions, verification and records, and to determine product shelf life using scientific data accounting for preservation treatments, hurdles and distribution/storage temperatures.

sections.section: 5.1.2.2 Development of scheduled heat or other treatments
sections.summary: Mandates scheduled heat or other treatments be designed to achieve the required log reduction of target microorganisms at the product's coldest point under worst-case conditions, taking into account factors (e.g. microbial load, pre-treatment growth potential, initial temperature, heat penetration, product composition, container size/fill limits, and recommended end‑user cooking), requires validation by a qualified person for any changes, and allows alternative approved technologies (e.g. HHP, irradiation) where authorized by the competent regulatory authority.

Specifies cooling must rapidly bring product to the specified storage temperature to minimize pathogen growth (avoiding prolonged times between 60°C and 10°C) and, when feasible, reach <10°C at the product center within two hours, allows scientifically justified alternative procedures, and lists factors to consider (initial product temperature, cooling medium and distribution, time, composition/viscosity, container/packaging and equipment capacity).

States other hurdles may be used to prevent or restrict pathogen growth but their effectiveness and synergy must be validated (e.g. challenge studies, predictive modelling), and when combined with heat or other treatments critical limits must be specified, measured, checked and recorded as necessary.

sections.section: 5.2 KEY ASPECTS OF HYGIENE CONTROL SYSTEMS
sections.summary: Describes key hygiene controls including time/temperature management to avoid prolonged exposure in the 10°C–60°C microbial growth range, obligations to maintain perishable materials refrigerated or ≥60°C during production delays, defined thawing procedures, quantified lethality and monitored delivery of heat/other treatments by trained personnel with calibrated recording devices and heat‑sensitive indicators, cooling monitoring and equipment control, and cold‑chain requirements including validation of storage temperatures, adherence to regulatory temperature criteria, FIFO stock rotation and regular temperature monitoring of storage, transport and display.

Refers to the General Principles of Food Hygiene and requires avoiding or rapidly passing through the 10°C–60°C growth range, maintaining perishable materials at temperatures that minimize growth or at ≥60°C during delays, and monitoring thawing, heat and other treatments by measuring time‑temperature relationships of the product or heating medium.

Requires thawing procedures be defined and strictly controlled by time and temperature to avoid conditions favorable to microbial multiplication, with immediate processing or refrigerated holding after thawing and adherence to microwave manufacturers' instructions to prevent uneven heating.

Requires quantification of lethality for heat and other treatments (e.g. using pasteurization or lethal rate values), performance by competent trained personnel, monitoring delivery by measuring time‑temperature of the product or heating medium, and use of monitoring/recording equipment that is regularly checked against accurate standards and maintained.

Mandates monitoring cooling by measuring time‑temperature of the product or cooling medium to reach prescribed relationships at the product's warmest point, requires cooling equipment to have monitoring devices and maintained records, and obliges regular calibration/verification of monitoring equipment to ensure conformity with the specified cooling method.

Requires continuous maintenance of the cold chain from packaging to consumption to preserve safety for stated shelf life, validation of selected storage temperatures, compliance with any regulatory temperature criteria (with shelf‑life re‑evaluation if temperatures differ), application of FIFO stock rotation, and regular monitoring of temperatures in storage, transport and display.
